title: Clarify Your Ideal Client
summary: Erin Stetzer led a session on defining ideal clients holistically — encompassing not just homeowners but also trades, architects, designers, and consultants — and how intentional positioning over time creates a supply that generates demand. The session transitioned into an open Q&A covering real participant challenges including homeowner rep positioning, transitioning away from smaller clients, managing out-of-budget designs, charging project management fees, and navigating a difficult returning client relationship. Practical frameworks for communication, budget management, and business growth at higher project tiers were discussed throughout.

teachings.explanation: Ideal clients are not just the people purchasing a home. They include trades, subcontractors, architects, designers, and consultants — everyone invited into your business ecosystem. Treating all of these people with respect and as clients creates a reputation that compounds over time.
teachings.quote: The clients aren't just the people purchasing. That's one part of a client. The clients are who we're actually inviting into our world and our team.

teachings.concept: Creating a Supply That People Demand
teachings.explanation: Rather than chasing clients, builders should deliberately build a supply — consisting of quality product, reliable people, and professional experiences — that naturally generates demand. This positioning accumulates over years and opens doors to higher-caliber projects.
teachings.quote: What we've done well, I think, is we create a supply that people demand. And what I mean by that is in our supply, we have product... we have people that can make promises and deliver on those promises.

teachings.concept: How You Do One Thing Is How You Do Everything
teachings.explanation: The standards and attention to detail a builder applies early in their career — even on small projects — establish the reputation and craft that enables them to compete for larger, more prestigious work later. Consistency in quality is a long-term positioning strategy.
teachings.quote: The builder I was 20 years ago, still analyzed grout lines. It drove me crazy that we could do something better and people weren't paying attention to it.

teachings.explanation: Subcontractors and trades should be treated as respected clients. When builders run organized, prepared job sites where trades can work efficiently and profitably, those trades spread word-of-mouth that becomes a powerful marketing tool with prospective clients.
teachings.quote: The trades say, when we're on a job, they're prepared. They have all the selections done. They have things laid out. They meet us on time. They don't waste my time. I actually make money on their projects.

teachings.concept: Sharing Your Expertise to Attract Clients
teachings.explanation: In order to be sought out for higher-level work or consulting roles, builders must proactively share how they solve problems and reduce risk — primarily through social media and within professional networks. Clients will not know your value unless you articulate it.
teachings.quote: I happened to share stuff on social media, and people were like, oh, well, maybe we should call her, just by talking. So every one of us should be using social media to share how we can help people.

teachings.explanation: Clients will not voluntarily attend budget meetings or engage with financial updates. It is the builder's responsibility to insist on regular, structured budget conversations. Without this, scope creep and surprises create conflict and erode trust.
teachings.quote: I will tell you that they, people will not volunteer for budget meetings. Including the ones that could easily spend a couple million with a conversation. So we are the leaders in that. It's insisting on it.

teachings.concept: Be a Trailblazer to Charge What You're Worth
teachings.explanation: If no one in your market charges project management fees or offers the level of reporting and transparency you provide, you must be willing to introduce and defend that model. Being different from the market is a feature, not a liability, when backed by demonstrated value.
teachings.quote: In order to be a trailblazer, you got to be a trailblazer. The reason you can put a project manager fee in there is because you can support it with all of what the project managers are doing.

teachings.concept: Ego Check Before Taking Difficult Clients
teachings.explanation: When a builder feels compelled to take on a previously toxic client relationship because they believe they can fix it, that impulse often stems from ego rather than sound business judgment. The honest test is whether you would be willing to absorb a lawsuit or make zero money on the project.
teachings.quote: I would challenge your ego a little bit. And I've been there where I can come in and save the day. If it is, I would be willing to walk into a lawsuit. I would be willing to make zero money. And then if I'm okay with those two things, I would take the project.

actionable_advice: Use social media consistently to share how you solve problems and reduce risk — this is how ideal clients find you rather than you chasing them.
When outgrowing past clients, communicate where your business is now rather than apologizing. Say 'here's what we're currently focused on' without framing it as a rejection.
Insist on regular budget meetings with clients on a weekly or biweekly cadence — clients will not initiate these conversations on their own.
Before starting any project, define what is required for successful delivery independent of the people involved, then have a conversation with the client about who will handle each component.
When a client wants to skip the design or planning phase to save money, walk them through the specific risks — including time, cost, and quality — using concrete tools like finish schedules.
All agreed-upon changes and budget modifications must be documented in writing after the verbal conversation is had; verbal agreements alone create legal and financial exposure.
Add project deliverables like weekly reports and four-week snapshots to your client contracts to differentiate your business and create demand.
Analyze project management fees every six months and adjust them based on actual hours and complexity — use current projects to calibrate future budget structures.

When a project is growing beyond its original scope or budget, call a formal timeout and schedule a dedicated sit-down meeting to walk clients through the full progression from start to current state.
When considering a partnership on a spec build, ensure profit on the land is clearly separated from profit on the build in the contract, and include downside protection clauses.
client_challenges: Martin asked how to position himself in a homeowner rep role when engaging with trades and prospective clients, and how to attract those clients.
Bennett is receiving calls from past clients for smaller projects he has outgrown, and is struggling with how to decline them respectfully without losing the relationship.
Julie has clients who resist paying for design or project management services and want to handle selections themselves, making it hard to justify those fees as part of the process.
Abby has a client without a designer who wants to do their own selections, and is navigating how to charge for the extra time that creates while keeping the project moving.

Abby is also managing a project where designs are being created in real time and are significantly over allowances, with a client who was disengaged from budget reviews until problems became unavoidable.

Ryan is finding that high-end boutique projects at the $2-2.5 million level require a disproportionate amount of supervision and project management hours compared to simpler builds, and project management fees are not yet standard in his market.

Renee is considering re-engaging a former client who threatened legal action over a photo shoot dispute, and is wrestling with whether better systems and boundaries can mitigate the risk of another toxic situation.
Renee has a separate returning client whose project paused for a year due to a family crisis, and now wants to move quickly despite signs of changing priorities and budget misalignment.
Rich asked at what price point prospective clients truly understand and accept the cost of luxury building without pushing for value engineering.
Martha is evaluating a spec build partnership with an established builder and seeking guidance on how to structure roles, responsibilities, and financial protections.
notable_quotes: On positioning over time: 'All of this positioning over years and years and years actually creates the next environment that we want to operate in.'
On declining past clients: 'I wouldn't even apologize for it actually. You haven't done anything wrong. And it's an honor to get that phone call — that means you're doing things right.'
On the homeowner rep business model: 'The clients we have don't have time. They have money. They don't have time and they don't have experts. That's what they're looking for. So be that.'

On taking on a difficult returning client: 'It sounds really toxic. I've been that person who wants to come in and I can do this and I can make it right. And I can be their friend. And I'm also a person of great faith. And it was my ego.'

On faith and passing on problem clients: 'Faith is not jumping in and being the hero. I have faith that because I made the right decision and didn't work on that, something better is coming up — and it happens every time.'
On introducing new fee structures in a traditional market: 'Nobody wants to be like everybody else. It just takes some guts to speak your truth. That's just what it is. And people feel it.'
